data.title: How is a micelle formed from amphiphilic polymers in a dialysis process: Insight from mesoscopic studies

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1016/j.cplett.2020.137711?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2020.137711, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Abstract Self-assembly of amphiphilic polymers into micelles in dialysis process is dynamic, and the phase transition process is therefore very challenging to observe experimentally. In this study, we built a dynamic modeling by applying dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) simulations to explore the entire micelle formation process during the dynamic. The self-assembled aggregates undergo four stages in the process of dialysis: (i) Dispersed stage, (ii) Pre-micellar stage, (iii) Over-micellar stage. (iv) The final micellar stage. The study thus provides great advantages in simulating the processing dynamics of drug delivery systems, which would further expand the application scope of DPD simulations.

data.title: Can we describe charged nanoparticles with electrolyte theories? Insight from mesoscopic simulation techniques

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.11722, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Abstract Electrolyte theories enable to describe the structural and dynamical properties of simple electrolytes in solution, such as sodium chloride in water. Using these theories for aqueous solutions of charged nanoparticles is a straightforward route to extract their charge and size from experimental data. Nevertheless, for such strongly asymmetric electrolytes, the validity of the underlying approximations have never been properly challenged with exact simulation results. In the present work, well established mesoscopic numerical simulations are used to challenge the ability of advanced electrolyte theories to predict the electrical conductivity of suspensions of charged nanoparticles, in the salt-free case. The theories under investigation are based on the Debye-Fuoss-Onsager treatment of electrolyte transport. When the nanoparticles are small enough (about 1 nm large), the theoretical results agree remarkably well with the simulation ones, even in the high concentration regime (volume fraction of nanoparticles larger than 3%). Strikingly, for highly charged nanoparticles, the theory is able to capture the non-monotonic variation of the ratio of the electrical conductivity to its value at infinite dilution (ideal value) as a function of the concentration. However, the tested theories fail to describe the conductivity of suspensions containing larger nanoparticles (e.g. of diameter 4 nm). Finally, only small charged nanoparticles can be considered as ions, as far as electrolyte theories are concerned.

data.title: From Molecular Insight to Mesoscale Membrane Remodeling: Curvature Generation by Arginine-Rich Cell-Penetrating Peptides

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1101/2025.04.14.648709?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.04.14.648709, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: The enhanced cell penetration ability of arginine-rich peptides, such as nonaarginine (R9), compared to their lysine-rich counterparts remains incompletely understood. Atomistic simulations reveal that R9 binds significantly more strongly (≈ 20 kJ/mol) and penetrates deeper into anionic lipid headgroup region than its lysine equivalent. This enhanced interaction translates into a stronger induction of negative membrane curvature by R9. We combine these data to construct a model of peptide binding and curvature induction in fusogenic lipid mixtures and employ a multiscale simulation approach, combining atomistic molecular dynamics (MD) with mesoscopic Monte Carlo (MC) simulations, to dissect the molecular basis and morphological consequences of arginine specificity. The results show that stable membrane invaginations, as observed in studies of cell penetration, require excess membrane and are stable only for R9 (outside of the domain of stability of the Helfrich stomatocyte). By analyzing lipid and protein sorting coupled to the membrane structure, we explain the interplay of Gaussian and mean curvature in providing a mechanistic basis for the initial membrane deformation events potentially involved in ‘Arginine Magic’ cell entry pathways.
